title: A bill to amend section 1151 of title 14, United States Code, to modify the restriction on construction of Coast Guard vessels in foreign shipyards.
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about where Coast Guard ships can be built. Right now, Coast Guard ships must be built in American shipyards. The new law would let the President approve building ships in foreign countries under special conditions. The foreign shipyard must be in a country that is friends with America, cost less money than American shipyards, and be able to build ships much faster. This would only happen when it helps keep America safe.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

sections.title: Section 1 Modification of Restriction on Construction of Vessels in Foreign Shipyards
sections.text: This section changes the rules about when the Coast Guard can have their ships built in shipyards in other countries instead of the United States. The President can now allow the Coast Guard to use foreign shipyards when it is important for America's national security. Before making this decision, the President must tell Congress and wait thirty days before any contract can be signed. The President can only approve using a foreign shipyard if several conditions are met. First, the foreign shipyard must be in a country that is either part of NATO (the North Atlantic Treaty Organization) or has a defense agreement with the United States in the Indo-Pacific region. Second, it must cost less money to build the ship in the foreign shipyard than in an American shipyard. Third, the foreign shipyard must be able to finish building and deliver the ship at least eighteen months faster than an American shipyard could. Fourth, the foreign shipyard must have proven in the past five years that they can build ships for Coast Guard missions cheaper and faster than American shipyards. The Coast Guard Secretary can also buy a ship that is already completely built from a foreign shipyard if the shipyard is in a NATO country or a country with a defense treaty in the Indo-Pacific region, and if that country's government agrees to provide a warranty for the ship.
